markdownRemark.html:
The values of NumberField and Currency are affected by certain properties, which can be divided into two cases:

  1. Directly changing the actual value of data interaction;
  2. Only changing the displayed value without affecting data interaction.

Related Properties

precision

Configuration for the number of decimal places to retain. This property directly affects the actual value of data interaction.

Configuration Priority

The precision property is supported at both the DS field configuration level and the component property configuration level. When used in combination with DS, the field configuration takes precedence over the component property configuration.

const ds = new DataSet({
  fields: [{name: 'count', type: 'number', defaultValue: 3.1415926, precision: 2}]
});
<NumberField dataSet={ds} name="count" precision={3} /> // Display value: 3.14
console.log(ds.toData()); // [{count: 3.14}]

Insufficient Decimal Places

  1. The actual value will not be padded with 0s;
  2. Without additional configuration, the value displayed in NumberField is consistent with the actual value;
  3. The value displayed in Currency will be padded with 0s up to the specified precision.
const ds = new DataSet({
  fields: [
    {name: 'count', type: 'number', defaultValue: 3.14, precision: 3},
    {name: 'money', type: 'number', defaultValue: 3.14, precision: 3, currency: 'CNY'},
  ]
});
<NumberField dataSet={ds} name="count" /> // Display value: 3.14
<Currency dataSet={ds} name="money" /> // Display value: ￥3.140
console.log(ds.toData()); // [{count: 3.14, money: 3.14}]

Removing Extra Zeros

When there are consecutive zeros at the end of the decimal part:

  1. The actual value will remove all invalid zeros;
  2. Without extra configuration, the displayed value of NumberField will be consistent with the actual value;
  3. The displayed value of Currency will retain decimals up to the specified precision.
const ds = new DataSet({
  fields: [
    {name: 'count', type: 'number', defaultValue: 3.140000, precision: 3},
    {name: 'money', type: 'number', defaultValue: 3.140000, precision: 3, currency: 'CNY'},
  ],
});
<NumberField dataSet={ds} name="count" /> // Displayed value: 3.14
<Currency dataSet={ds} name="money" /> // Displayed value: ￥3.140
console.log(ds.toData()); // [{count: 3.14, money: 3.14}]

Decimal Places Exceeding Precision

Excess digits will be truncated according to the 'round half up' rule.

const ds = new DataSet({
  fields: [
    {name: 'count', type: 'number', defaultValue: 3.1415926, precision: 3},
    {name: 'money', type: 'number', defaultValue: 3.1415926, precision: 3, currency: 'CNY'},
  ],
});
<NumberField dataSet={ds} name="count" /> // Displayed value: 3.142
<Currency dataSet={ds} name="money" /> // Displayed value: ￥3.142
console.log(ds.toData()); // [{count: 3.142, money: 3.142}]

formatterOptions

Number and currency formatting configuration.

Basic Principle

This property is mainly based on the Intl.NumberFormat object to format numbers according to a specific locale.

The decimal part is primarily formatted using the minimumFractionDigits and maximumFractionDigits properties in formatterOptions.options to set the minimum and maximum number of decimal places.

Features

This property only formats the displayed value and does not change the actual value used in data interactions.

const ds = new DataSet({
  fields: [
    {
      name: 'count',
      type: 'number',
      defaultValue: 3.1415926,
      formatterOptions: {
        options: { maximumFractionDigits: 3 }
      },
    },
    {
      name: 'money',
      type: 'number',
      defaultValue: 3.1415926,
      formatterOptions: {
        options: { maximumFractionDigits: 3 }
      },
      currency: 'CNY'
    },
  ],
});
<NumberField dataSet={ds} name="count" /> // Display value: 3.142
<Currency dataSet={ds} name="money" /> // Display value: ¥3.142
console.log(ds.toData()); // [{count: 3.1415926, money: 3.1415926}]

Scenario Explanation

When only maximumFractionDigits is set:

  1. If the decimal places are insufficient, NumberField will not pad with 0, while Currency will pad the decimal part according to the currency;
  2. When the decimal part ends with consecutive 0s, NumberField will truncate all redundant 0s, while Currency will handle this according to the currency;
  3. If the number of decimal places exceeds the limit, the extra part will be truncated following the 'round half up' rule.

Related Properties

  • numberFieldFormatter NumberField formatter;
  • numberFieldFormatterOptions NumberField formatter options;
  • currencyFormatter Currency formatter;
  • currencyFormatterOptions Currency formatter options.

Step

By clicking the step buttons, the actual value changes, which can trigger some formatting changes.

  1. The value will not be automatically modified or formatted, and abnormal values will trigger a warning;
  2. Truncation does not follow the 'round half up' rule; it first 'truncates,' then adds or subtracts the step;
  3. Enabling nonStrictStep and then clicking the step button will change the actual value, but it will not apply the truncation-like formatting and will not trigger an abnormal value warning.
const ds = new DataSet({
  fields: [
    {name: 'count', type: 'number', defaultValue: 3.1415926, step: 0.001},
    {name: 'money', type: 'number', defaultValue: 3.1415926, step: 0.001, currency: 'CNY'},

    {name: 'nonStrictStepCount', type: 'number', defaultValue: 3.1415926, step: 0.001, nonStrictStep: true},
    {name: 'nonStrictStepMoney', type: 'number', defaultValue: 3.1415926, step: 0.001, nonStrictStep: true, currency: 'CNY'},
  ],
});
<NumberField dataSet={ds} name="count" /> // Display value: 3.1415926
<Currency dataSet={ds} name="money" /> // Display value: ¥3.14
<NumberField dataSet={ds} name="nonStrictStepCount" /> // Display value: 3.1415926
<Currency dataSet={ds} name="nonStrictStepMoney" /> // Display value: ¥3.14
console.log(ds.toData()); // [{count: 3.1415926, money: 3.1415926, nonStrictStepCount: 3.1415926, nonStrictStepMoney: 3.1415926}]
// Increase by one step
<NumberField dataSet={ds} name="count" /> // Display value: 3.142
<Currency dataSet={ds} name="money" /> // Display value: ¥3.14
<NumberField dataSet={ds} name="nonStrictStepCount" /> // Display value: 3.1425926
<Currency dataSet={ds} name="nonStrictStepMoney" /> // Display value: ¥3.14
console.log(ds.toData()); // [{count: 3.142, money: 3.142, nonStrictStepCount: 3.1425926, nonStrictStepMoney: 3.1425926}]

Combined Usage

precision & formatterOptions

Property Relationship

  1. When processing actual values, precision works independently and is not related to formatterOptions;
  2. When formatting displayed values: precision is applied to both minimumFractionDigits and maximumFractionDigits, but it has lower priority compared to the field property formatterOptions and the global property numberFieldFormatterOption.

Scenario Description

  1. precision > maximumFractionDigits:

    Ⅰ. Actual values change according to precision;

    Ⅱ. Displayed values are formatted according to maximumFractionDigits;

    Ⅲ. Currency will throw an error.

const ds = new DataSet({
  fields: [
    {
      name: 'count',
      type: 'number',
      defaultValue: 0.123456789,
      precision: 9,
      formatterOptions: {
        options: { maximumFractionDigits: 3 }
      },
},
    {
      name: 'money',
      type: 'number',
      defaultValue: 0.123456789,
      precision: 9,
      formatterOptions: {
        options: { maximumFractionDigits: 3 }
      },
      currency: 'CNY'
    },
  ],
});
<NumberField dataSet={ds} name="count" /> // Displayed value: 0.123
<Currency dataSet={ds} name="money" /> // RangeError: maximumFractionDigits value is out of range.
console.log(ds.toData()); // [{count: 0.123456789}]

  1. precision < maximumFractionDigits:

    I. The actual value changes according to precision;

    II. maximumFractionDigits is meaningless. Precision will make the actual value's decimal places fewer than maximumFractionDigits, preventing it from achieving the formatting effect.

const ds = new DataSet({
  fields: [
    {
      name: 'count',
      type: 'number',
      defaultValue: 0.123456789,
      precision: 3,
      formatterOptions: {
        options: { maximumFractionDigits: 9 }
      },
    },
    {
      name: 'money',
      type: 'number',
      defaultValue: 0.123456789,
      precision: 3,
      formatterOptions: {
        options: { maximumFractionDigits: 9 }
      },
      currency: 'CNY'
    },
  ],
});
<NumberField dataSet={ds} name="count" /> // Display value: 0.123
<Currency dataSet={ds} name="money" /> // Display value: ￥0.123
console.log(ds.toData()); // [{count: 0.123, money: 0.123}]

precision & step

Before clicking the step button: the actual value (default/input value) follows the precision setting.

After clicking the step button:

  1. If precision > step decimal places: the actual value changes, showing insufficient decimal places according to precision;

  2. If precision < step decimal places: exceeding decimal places according to precision:

    I. When ± step causes the actual value and precision triggers rounding "down", the actual value does not change;

    II. When ± step causes the actual value and precision triggers rounding "up", the actual value changes, but there may be a deviation from the expected step calculation.

formatterOptions & step

Before clicking the step button:

  1. The actual value (default/input value) does not change;
  2. The displayed value is formatted according to formatterOptions.

After clicking the step button: the actual value changes.

  1. If maximumFractionDigits > step decimal places: insufficient decimal places according to formatterOptions;
  2. If maximumFractionDigits < step decimal places: exceeding decimal places according to formatterOptions.

Notes

  1. Precision affects minimumFractionDigits and maximumFractionDigits. Not meeting the standard conditions can cause a formatting error.
  2. When precision > maximumFractionDigits, currency types may throw errors because minimumFractionDigits > maximumFractionDigits.
  3. For plain number types, precision does not affect minimumFractionDigits (the minimum number of decimal places, default is 0), so it will not pad or retain zeros, whereas currency types behave differently.
  4. For different currencies, the default values of minimumFractionDigits and maximumFractionDigits vary. See the official documentation for details.

Give it a try

You can use the following examples to verify the above points.

pageContext.html:
The values of NumberField and Currency are affected by certain properties, which can be divided into two cases:

  1. Directly changing the actual value of data interaction;
  2. Only changing the displayed value without affecting data interaction.

Related Properties

precision

Configuration for the number of decimal places to retain. This property directly affects the actual value of data interaction.

Configuration Priority

  fields: [{name: 'count', type: 'number', defaultValue: 3.1415926, precision: 2}]

console.log(ds.toData()); // [{count: 3.14}]

Insufficient Decimal Places

  2. Without additional configuration, the value displayed in NumberField is consistent with the actual value;
  3. The value displayed in Currency will be padded with 0s up to the specified precision.

<Currency dataSet={ds} name="money" /> // Display value: ￥3.140

Removing Extra Zeros

When there are consecutive zeros at the end of the decimal part:

  2. Without extra configuration, the displayed value of NumberField will be consistent with the actual value;
  3. The displayed value of Currency will retain decimals up to the specified precision.

  2. If maximumFractionDigits < step decimal places: exceeding decimal places according to formatterOptions.

Notes

  1. Precision affects minimumFractionDigits and maximumFractionDigits. Not meeting the standard conditions can cause a formatting error.
  2. When precision > maximumFractionDigits, currency types may throw errors because minimumFractionDigits > maximumFractionDigits.
  3. For plain number types, precision does not affect minimumFractionDigits (the minimum number of decimal places, default is 0), so it will not pad or retain zeros, whereas currency types behave differently.
  4. For different currencies, the default values of minimumFractionDigits and maximumFractionDigits vary. See the official documentation for details.
